verses.text: Now it came to pass upon a day, that Jonathan the son of Saul said unto the young man that bare his armour, Come, and let us go over to the Philistines' garrison, that is on the other side. But he told not his father. And Saul tarried in the uttermost part of Gibeah under a pomegranate tree which is in Migron: and the people that were with him were about six hundred men; And Ahiah, the son of Ahitub, I-chabod's brother, the son of Phinehas, the son of Eli, the LORD's priest in Shiloh, wearing an ephod. And the people knew not that Jonathan was gone. Guidance: The contrast is the engine of the chapter. Saul sits 'in the uttermost part of Gibeah under a pomegranate tree' — a stationary king, in the shade, surrounded by six hundred frightened men. Jonathan, by contrast, is moving — and moving secretly. 'He told not his father.' The genealogy on the priest is doing real work too: Ahiah is Phinehas's grandson, great-grandson of Eli, brother of Ichabod ('the glory is departed') — the priesthood at Saul's court is the cursed Shiloh line, traceable back to Eli's failed house. The two failed houses of the early monarchy, Eli and Saul, share a tent. God is not going to use the priest in the tent or the king under the tree; He is about to use the prince who slipped away. Initiative is one of God's preferred sites for grace.

And between the passages, by which Jonathan sought to go over unto the Philistines' garrison, there was a sharp rock on the one side, and a sharp rock on the other side: and the name of the one was Bozez, and the name of the other Seneh. The forefront of the one was situate northward over against Michmash, and the other southward over against Gibeah. Guidance: The narrator pauses to give us the precise topography because the geography is theology. The Michmash pass is a deep wadi with cliff-faces of nearly four hundred feet on either side; the two prominent outcrops have names — Bozez ('shining,' the south-facing one that catches the sun) and Seneh ('thorny'). Modern travelers have identified them; the cliffs are still there. The Philistine garrison sits on top of the northern cliff. Jonathan and his armor-bearer will have to descend one cliff and climb the other, in full view of an enemy outpost, with no possibility of retreat once they begin the climb. The narrator slows down here so we feel exactly what Jonathan is about to attempt. God's most striking deliverances often run through terrain that on paper looks impossible.

And Jonathan said to the young man that bare his armour, Come, and let us go over unto the garrison of these uncircumcised: it may be that the LORD will work for us: for there is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few. And his armourbearer said unto him, Do all that is in thine heart: turn thee; behold, I am with thee according to thy heart. Guidance: Two men, one armed, one with a borrowed weapon, against a fortified Philistine outpost — and Jonathan says one of the great sentences of faith in Scripture: 'there is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few.' Notice the 'it may be.' Jonathan is not claiming a private revelation; he is acting on the *theology of who God is*. The LORD has saved by 300 (Gideon), by 600 (David's later band), by individuals (Ehud, Shamgar). Numbers are not what the deliverance depends on. The armor-bearer's reply is one of the great loyalty lines: 'I am with thee according to thy heart' — wherever your heart goes, my arms follow. Faith and friendship work the same way: one person believes God enough to move, and one person believes the believer enough to come along. Both are needed; both are honored.

Then said Jonathan, Behold, we will pass over unto these men, and we will discover ourselves unto them. If they say thus unto us, Tarry until we come to you; then we will stand still in our place, and will not go up unto them. But if they say thus, Come up unto us; then we will go up: for the LORD hath delivered them into our hand: and this shall be a sign unto us. Guidance: Jonathan asks for a sign — but the sign is not arbitrary. He is reading military psychology theologically. If the Philistines say 'wait, we'll come down,' that means they are coming out to engage on level ground — they have initiative. If they say 'come up,' it means they are too lazy or contemptuous to descend — they expect Jonathan to do the hard climbing while they sit. Their *contempt* will be the signal that God has delivered them. Jonathan is asking God to make the enemy's pride the proof of the LORD's plan. Notice that he commits in advance: either response means a definite next action, not a 'let's think it over.' Faith builds yes-or-no fences before the moment of pressure, so the pressure does not bend the commitment.

And both of them discovered themselves unto the garrison of the Philistines: and the Philistines said, Behold, the Hebrews come forth out of the holes where they had hid themselves. And the men of the garrison answered Jonathan and his armourbearer, and said, Come up to us, and we will shew you a thing. And Jonathan said unto his armourbearer, Come up after me: for the LORD hath delivered them into the hand of Israel. Guidance: The sign lands exactly as Jonathan hoped. The Philistine taunt — 'Hebrews crawling out of their holes' — refers to 13:6, where the people had hidden in caves and pits; the occupier has been mocking the cowardice of Israel and now thinks Jonathan and his armor-bearer are just two more frightened men to amuse them. 'Come up to us, and we will shew you a thing' — the word 'thing' (dabar) probably has the menacing tone of 'we'll teach you a lesson.' Jonathan reads it as the sign: 'the LORD hath delivered them into the hand of Israel.' Note the certainty has hardened — 'it may be' (v6) becomes 'hath delivered' (v12). Faith does not have to start with certainty; it grows certainty as it obeys. The Philistines are issuing an invitation to their own funeral and do not know it.

And Jonathan climbed up upon his hands and upon his feet, and his armourbearer after him: and they fell before Jonathan; and his armourbearer slew after him. And that first slaughter, which Jonathan and his armourbearer made, was about twenty men, within as it were an half acre of land, which a yoke of oxen might plow. Guidance: 'Upon his hands and upon his feet' — Jonathan climbs the four-hundred-foot cliff face on all fours, sword presumably slung, exposed to anyone above with a stone or spear, then arrives at the top in fighting condition. His armor-bearer follows, finishing off whomever Jonathan knocks down. The narrator gives a striking domestic measurement: 'about an half acre of land, which a yoke of oxen might plow' — the slaughter happens in a tiny space, the size of a single field a farm pair could turn in a morning. Two men kill twenty in a patch of ground the size of a yard. The detail is not gore; it is precision — the LORD does the impossible in a footprint so small that no one can claim it was a battlefield they happened to win.

And there was trembling in the host, in the field, and among all the people: the garrison, and the spoilers, they also trembled, and the earth quaked: so it was a very great trembling. Guidance: A single verse and the whole Philistine army comes apart. Notice the layers of 'trembling': the host, the field, the people, the garrison, the spoilers — and underneath them all, the ground itself. 'The earth quaked' — God adds a literal tremor to the psychological one. The Hebrew word for the trembling at the end of the verse, herdat elohim, is sometimes translated 'a panic from God' — the same kind of supernatural terror Jacob's God sent ahead of him in Genesis 35. Jonathan and his armor-bearer killed twenty men; God did the rest. The fall of the Philistine line is not a tactical victory but a divine intervention through a two-man entry point. When the LORD chooses to act, He uses someone's small obedience as the door and walks an army through it.

And the watchmen of Saul in Gibeah of Benjamin looked; and, behold, the multitude melted away, and they went on beating down one another. Then said Saul unto the people that were with him, Number now, and see who is gone from us. And when they had numbered, behold, Jonathan and his armourbearer were not there. Guidance: Saul's watchmen — back at Gibeah, miles south — see the unmistakable spectacle of an enemy army collapsing on itself. The Hebrew is vivid: the multitude 'melted away' (literally 'dissolved') and 'beating down one another' (they are striking their own ranks in the panic — 14:20 will confirm this). Saul realizes something has happened that he did not order and does not understand. He calls a roll. Jonathan and his armor-bearer are missing. Saul learns about the victory by realizing his son is not where he is supposed to be. A leader who is genuinely close to his people knows when one is missing before counting; Saul has to count. The distance between Saul and Jonathan in this chapter is not just physical.

And Saul said unto Ahiah, Bring hither the ark of God. For the ark of God was at that time with the children of Israel. And it came to pass, while Saul talked unto the priest, that the noise that was in the host of the Philistines went on and increased: and Saul said unto the priest, Withdraw thine hand. Guidance: Saul tries to inquire of God — and immediately interrupts himself when he hears the battle noise growing. The Septuagint and many translations read 'ephod' rather than 'ark' here, which fits better (the ark was at Kiriath-jearim per 7:1-2 and would not have been at Gibeah). Either way, Saul has the priest mid-consultation when he decides he has no time for God's answer and tells him to put his hand back. The contrast with Jonathan is excruciating: Jonathan acted *on* his theology of God; Saul tries to consult and then aborts the consultation because the battle is too loud. The king who broke the seven-day waiting commandment for the same reason — 'no time for proper inquiry, attack now' — is breaking the same pattern again, even more rapidly. Sin learned does not retire; it deepens its groove.

And Saul and all the people that were with him assembled themselves, and they came to the battle: and, behold, every man's sword was against his fellow, and there was a very great discomfiture. Moreover the Hebrews that were with the Philistines before that time, which went up with them into the camp from the country round about, even they also turned to be with the Israelites that were with Saul and Jonathan. Likewise all the men of Israel which had hid themselves in mount Ephraim, when they heard that the Philistines fled, even they also followed hard after them in the battle. So the LORD saved Israel that day: and the battle passed over unto Beth-aven. Guidance: Three groups join the battle. First, Saul's six hundred. Second, the Hebrew mercenaries who had been pressed into Philistine service — they turn coat the moment they see the panic, and now fight the army they were embedded in. Third, the men of Israel who had hidden in mount Ephraim's caves — once they hear the fleeing rumor, they pour out of the rocks. The narrator's verdict is unmissable: 'So the LORD saved Israel that day.' Not Saul, not Jonathan alone, not the turned mercenaries — the LORD. The victory belongs to the One whose theology Jonathan trusted in v6. Notice the geography too: the battle 'passed over unto Beth-aven,' moving the action westward toward the coastal plain, reversing the Philistine penetration of chapter 13. God turns the map back to where it should be.

And the men of Israel were distressed that day: for Saul had adjured the people, saying, Cursed be the man that eateth any food until evening, that I may be avenged on mine enemies. So none of the people tasted any food. Guidance: Saul has imposed an oath: no one eats until evening. The motive he gives — 'that I may be avenged on mine enemies' — is the tell. 'My enemies,' not 'the LORD's enemies.' The pursuit has stopped being about delivering Israel and started being about personal vindication. The oath sounds religious (it is a vow of fasting); it is in fact narcissistic. The result is military disaster: men who have been fighting and pursuing since dawn are forbidden food, and they are 'distressed' — the Hebrew is 'pressed hard,' faint to the point of collapse. Saul's rash piety is dismantling the very victory it was meant to consummate. The chapter's recurring lesson: undisciplined religious gestures from the throne wound the army the throne is supposed to lead.

And all they of the land came to a wood; and there was honey upon the ground. And when the people were come into the wood, behold, the honey dropped; but no man put his hand to his mouth: for the people feared the oath. But Jonathan heard not when his father charged the people with the oath: wherefore he put forth the end of the rod that was in his hand, and dipped it in an honeycomb, and put his hand to his mouth; and his eyes were enlightened. Guidance: The army enters a wood where wild honey is literally on the ground — combs in tree hollows have overflowed and dripped. It is a perfect emergency ration; God has, in effect, set a banquet for an exhausted army. But not one man eats. They fear the oath more than they trust their bodies. Jonathan, who was not present when the oath was sworn (he had been miles away on the cliff), reaches his staff into a comb and lifts honey to his mouth. The text says 'his eyes were enlightened' — the strength returned to him so fast it was visible in his face. Honey is a high-glycemic shot of energy; the body shows it within minutes. The verse stages a contrast: God has provided food in the wood, the king has forbidden food by oath, and the only person eating is the one who didn't hear the oath. The provision-of-God versus the constraint-of-king tension is going to come to a head.

Then answered one of the people, and said, Thy father straitly charged the people with an oath, saying, Cursed be the man that eateth any food this day. And the people were faint. And Jonathan said, My father hath troubled the land: see, I pray you, how mine eyes have been enlightened, because I tasted a little of this honey. How much more, if haply the people had eaten freely to day of the spoil of their enemies which they found? for had there not been now a much greater slaughter among the Philistines? Guidance: Someone explains to Jonathan what his father has decreed. Jonathan's reply is one of the bluntest critiques a son ever offers a father in scripture: 'my father hath troubled the land.' 'Troubled' (akar) is the same word Joshua used about Achan in Joshua 7 — the man whose private disobedience brought defeat on Israel. Jonathan is saying: my father has functioned like Achan, but from the throne. He makes the empirical case too: 'how much more if the people had eaten freely?' A nourished army would have completed the rout; a faint army stops at sundown. Saul's piety has bought him a victory at half-strength. Sometimes the most damaging thing a leader does is impose a discipline on his people that gets in the way of God's own provision for them.

Then answered one of the people, and said, Thy father straitly charged the people with an oath, saying, Cursed be the man that eateth any food this day. And the people were faint. And Jonathan said, My father hath troubled the land: see, I pray you, how mine eyes have been enlightened, because I tasted a little of this honey. How much more, if haply the people had eaten freely to day of the spoil of their enemies which they found? for had there not been now a much greater slaughter among the Philistines? A nourished army would have completed the rout; a faint army stops at sundown. Saul's piety has bought him a victory at half-strength. Sometimes the most damaging thing a leader does is impose a discipline on his people that gets in the way of God's own provision for them.

And they smote the Philistines that day from Michmash to Aijalon: and the people were very faint. And the people flew upon the spoil, and took sheep, and oxen, and calves, and slew them on the ground: and the people did eat them with the blood. Then they told Saul, saying, Behold, the people sin against the LORD, in that they eat with the blood. And he said, Ye have transgressed: roll a great stone unto me this day. And Saul said, Disperse yourselves among the people, and say unto them, Bring me hither every man his ox, and every man his sheep, and slay them here, and eat; and sin not against the LORD in eating with the blood: and bring every man his ox with him that night, and slew them there. And Saul built an altar unto the LORD: the same was the first altar that he built unto the LORD. Guidance: The pursuit covers nearly twenty miles, from Michmash all the way to Aijalon — the Philistines fleeing west, Saul's army limping after them. At sundown the oath expires; the famished army falls on the spoil, butchers livestock and eats the meat 'with the blood' (Lev 17:10–14 forbids this; the blood must be drained before consumption). Saul, told of it, rolls a great stone for proper slaughter and orders disciplined butchering: animals brought to the stone, blood drained, meat eaten cleanly. Then a footnote with weight: 'the same was the first altar that he built unto the LORD.' Saul is two years into his reign. The crisis required a stone for kosher slaughter; he turned the stone into an altar. The detail is unflattering — his first altar to the LORD was reactive, not proactive — but it is also not nothing. Sometimes the most religious gesture of a backsliding leader is the one he is finally pressed into by necessity.

And Saul said, Let us go down after the Philistines by night, and spoil them until the morning light, and let us not leave a man of them. And they said, Do whatsoever seemeth good unto thee. Then said the priest, Let us draw near hither unto God. And Saul asked counsel of God, Shall I go down after the Philistines? wilt thou deliver them into the hand of Israel? But he answered him not that day. Guidance: Now Saul wants to inquire — too late. The priest reminds him to consult God before launching the night pursuit (the priest, not Saul, raises the question — a quiet judgment on the king). Saul prays. The LORD does not answer. The silence is itself the answer: something has broken the line of communication. Saul rightly diagnoses that there must be sin in the camp blocking the inquiry. He is theologically correct about *what is wrong*; he is about to be tragically wrong about *who is responsible*. The very oath Saul himself imposed is what has cursed the camp — but Saul cannot see his own decree as the source of the problem. Leaders who cannot suspect themselves invariably end up suspecting the wrong people.

And Saul said, Draw ye near hither, all the chief of the people: and know and see wherein this sin hath been this day. For, as the LORD liveth, which saveth Israel, though it be in Jonathan my son, he shall surely die. But there was not a man among all the people that answered him. Guidance: Saul, called to investigate, doubles down on the oath that caused the trouble in the first place. 'Though it be in Jonathan my son, he shall surely die.' He stakes his religious sincerity on the willingness to kill his own son if necessary — and he is solemn, not theatrical. But notice the army's silence: 'there was not a man that answered.' They know what happened. They are not going to volunteer information that will execute the day's hero. The silence is the army's verdict on Saul's oath. Bad religious oaths sometimes have to be unmade not by argument but by the steady refusal of God's people to enforce them.

Then said he unto all Israel, Be ye on one side, and I and Jonathan my son will be on the other side. And the people said unto Saul, Do what seemeth good unto thee. And Saul said unto the LORD God of Israel, Give a perfect lot. And Saul and Jonathan were taken: but the people escaped. And Saul said, Cast lots between me and Jonathan my son. And Jonathan was taken. Guidance: Lots are cast — the Urim and Thummim in the priest's ephod, the binary yes/no oracle of Israel. The procedure is escalation by elimination: people versus king-and-prince, then king versus prince. Each draw narrows the target. Notice: the LORD answers Saul's lot inquiry though He had not answered Saul's military inquiry — God will play the game Saul has set up, knowing the army will refuse to let it end the way Saul intends. The narrative is doing two things at once: confirming that yes, Jonathan tasted the honey, and confirming that Saul's oath is exposed before all Israel. Jonathan stands taken; the room knows what comes next.

Then Saul said to Jonathan, Tell me what thou hast done. And Jonathan told him, and said, I did but taste a little honey with the end of the rod that was in mine hand, and, lo, I must die. And Saul answered, God do so and more also: for thou shalt surely die, Jonathan. Guidance: Jonathan's confession is straightforward and self-aware. 'I did but taste a little honey… and, lo, I must die.' There is no defense, no plea, no protest of the unfairness of the oath he never heard. He simply states the facts and accepts the consequence. The note of wry resignation — 'and, lo, I must die' — is the dignity of a son who refuses to argue with his father in front of the army. Saul, however, doubles down a third time: 'God do so and more also: for thou shalt surely die.' This is the oath formula at maximum severity. The chapter has reached the cliff edge: a king on the verge of executing the army's hero, his own son, to satisfy his own oath. It is the great pivot. The army is about to step in.

The chapter has reached the cliff edge: a king on the verge of executing the army's hero, his own son, to satisfy his own oath. It is the great pivot. The army is about to step in.

And the people said unto Saul, Shall Jonathan die, who hath wrought this great salvation in Israel? God forbid: as the LORD liveth, there shall not one hair of his head fall to the ground; for he hath wrought with God this day. So the people rescued Jonathan, that he died not. Guidance: The army interposes — 'shall Jonathan die, who hath wrought this great salvation?' They counter-oath the king: 'as the LORD liveth, not one hair of his head shall fall.' The phrase 'wrought with God' is the army's verdict on the day: it was God's deliverance done through Jonathan, and you do not execute the LORD's instrument for the king's bad vow. 'So the people rescued Jonathan.' The verb is strong; they ransomed him, redeemed him out of the king's hand. This is one of the few times in the Hebrew Bible when the army restrains its own king — a healthy moment of constitutional pushback. The 'man after God's own heart' promised in chapter 13 was a future king; this is something narrower but precious: an army that knows the difference between a bad oath and a faithful obedience. Bad oaths should not be kept; broken bad oaths grieve God less than the bloodshed they would cause.

Then Saul went up from following the Philistines: and the Philistines went to their own place. Guidance: A one-verse coda to the day's events. Saul stops the pursuit. The Philistines walk home unfinished. The night pursuit Saul wanted earlier never happens because the lot ritual and the near-execution have consumed the time and energy. Verse 30 already warned us — a faint army cannot finish what a fed army would have — and the rest of the chapter confirmed Jonathan's diagnosis. The unfinished business will recur all through Saul's reign. The Philistines do not get crushed today; they retreat to fight again. Saul's reign is permanently shaped by this pattern: God-given openings that human folly leaves half-exploited. The contrast with David later — who will press deliverances to completion — is being set up in real time.

So Saul took the kingdom over Israel, and fought against all his enemies on every side, against Moab, and against the children of Ammon, and against Edom, and against the kings of Zobah, and against the Philistines: and whithersoever he turned himself, he vexed them. And he gathered an host, and smote the Amalekites, and delivered Israel out of the hands of them that spoiled them. Guidance: A summary verse that telescopes years of Saul's reign. Despite the chapter's failures, Saul is not a failed king on every dimension. He fights and wins against the surrounding peoples — Moab to the southeast, Ammon further east, Edom to the south, the Zobah kingdoms to the north, the Philistines to the west. 'Whithersoever he turned himself, he vexed them' — he gave them all hard fights. The summary is unironic; Saul really did secure Israel against the regional powers in a way that the judges had been unable to do, and the standing army he created made it possible. The narrator is keeping the picture honest: Saul is not a useless king. He is a king who could have been great, who built real things, but who repeatedly cannot pass the obedience test. The dynasty falls not because the man was incompetent but because he was not 'after God's own heart.'

Now the sons of Saul were Jonathan, and Ishui, and Melchi-shua: and the names of his two daughters were these; the name of the firstborn Merab, and the name of the younger Michal: And the name of Saul's wife was Ahinoam, the daughter of Ahimaaz: and the name of the captain of his host was Abner, the son of Ner, Saul's uncle. And Kish was the father of Saul; and Ner the father of Abner was the son of Abiel. Guidance: A genealogical inventory of the Saulide royal house, important for what is coming. Three sons (Jonathan, Ishui [also called Abinadab, 31:2], Melchi-shua — Ish-bosheth is omitted here, perhaps because he is too young or born later); two daughters, Merab and Michal — both will be married off to David; the wife Ahinoam (an interesting coincidence: David later has a wife named Ahinoam as well, possibly the same woman, possibly not); and the cousin/commander Abner, son of Saul's uncle Ner — the family power-broker, who will outlive Saul and try to install Ish-bosheth on the throne. The list is not decorative; it names the chess pieces that will move across the rest of the book. The royal family is now a known quantity, and the narrative is positioning everyone for the entry of an outsider — David — who is about to disturb every relationship in this list.

And there was sore war against the Philistines all the days of Saul: and when Saul saw any strong man, or any valiant man, he took him unto him. Guidance: The chapter's closing summary. The Philistine war was not won at Michmash; it 'was sore… all the days of Saul.' The threat that defined his reign never went away. The second half of the sentence is a quiet setup for chapter 16: 'when Saul saw any strong man, or any valiant man, he took him unto him.' Saul has been actively recruiting talent into his court — and in two chapters he will recruit a shepherd boy who plays a harp and slings stones, not realizing he is hiring his own successor. The narrator drops the line as a hinge: the very policy that strengthened Saul's reign is the door David will walk through. God often arranges the entrance of his chosen people through routes the previous regime itself built. Saul's recruitment program will deliver him the one man whose presence the throne cannot survive.
